---
title: "Case Study 2: Technical Presentation"
subtitle: "STAT 946 – Case Studies in Data Science"
author:
  - "Dario Greco"
  - "Lovera Lokeswara"
  - "Sanika Poojary"
  - "Sophia Feng"
date: "March 9, 2026"
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: dolphin
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 10pt
    incremental: false
    number-sections: false
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{siunitx}
  - \newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
  - \newcommand{\Var}{\mathrm{Var}}
---

# Comparison and evaluation

## Model Performance - Forecast

![Toronto PM2.5 4-Hour Forecast Model Comparison](../forecast.png){width="80%"}

---

## Model Performance - RMSE Comparison

| Model  | RMSE   |
|--------|--------|
| LASSO  | 6.7843 |
| N-HiTS | 6.7347 |

: Model RMSE Comparison {#tbl-model-rmse}

- The LASSO and N-HiTS models produce very similar RMSE values, indicating that much of the predictive signal is captured by linear relationships and recent pollution history.

- The small improvement from N-HiTS suggests that nonlinear temporal structure exists but contributes only marginal additional predictive power for a 4-hour forecast horizon.

## Overall Forecasting Accuracy and Effectiveness 

- Typical hourly PM$_{2.5}$ levels in Ontario often range from ~5–30 µg/m³, with occasional spikes above 50 µg/m³ during pollution events. An RMSE of ~6.7 µg/m³ therefore corresponds to an average prediction error of roughly 20–30% of typical concentration levels.

- This level of error is reasonable for short-term environmental forecasting, where pollution levels are highly variable and influenced by complex atmospheric processes.

- This forecasting system should be treated as an early-warning decision support tool, not a precise measurement system.

- Its value lies in anticipating pollution spikes early enough for operational adjustments, rather than predicting exact PM$_{2.5}$ concentrations. 


## Appendix: additional figures

Include:

TBD
